title: How do Organisms Reproduce?
summary: Chapter 7 covers reproduction in living organisms — both asexual and sexual. Asexual reproduction includes fission, budding, fragmentation, regeneration, vegetative propagation, and spore formation. Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of male and female gametes. Reproduction in flowering plants (pollination, fertilisation) and in humans (male and female reproductive systems, menstrual cycle, contraception) are studied in detail.
learningPoints: Define asexual reproduction and list its types: fission, budding, fragmentation, regeneration, vegetative propagation, spore formation
Explain vegetative propagation with examples and its advantages
Explain reproduction by spores in fungi and ferns
Define sexual reproduction and explain why it creates variation
Describe parts of a flower: sepals, petals, stamens (male), carpels (female)
Describe fertilisation in flowers: pollen tube growth, double fertilisation
Explain changes in flower after fertilisation: seed and fruit formation
Describe the male reproductive system: testes, vas deferens, prostate gland, penis
Describe the female reproductive system: ovaries, fallopian tube, uterus
Explain menstrual cycle and its significance
List methods of contraception: mechanical, oral, surgical

formulas.label: Regeneration
formulas.description: Planaria can be cut into pieces — each piece regenerates into a complete organism.

formulas.label: Vegetative Propagation
formulas.expression: Plant part → new plant without seeds
formulas.description: Roots, stems, leaves can produce new plants. Examples: potato (stem), rose (stem cutting), Bryophyllum (leaves).

diagrams.title: Structure of a Flower
diagrams.description: Sepal (protect bud), Petal (attract pollinators), Stamen (anther + filament = male), Carpel (stigma + style + ovary = female). Ovary contains ovules.

diagrams.title: Human Male Reproductive System
diagrams.description: Testes (sperm + testosterone), Epididymis (sperm maturation), Vas deferens (transport), Seminal vesicle, Prostate gland (fluid medium), Urethra, Penis.

diagrams.title: Human Female Reproductive System
diagrams.description: Ovaries (eggs + estrogen), Fallopian tube (egg transport, fertilisation), Uterus (implantation, foetal development), Vagina (birth canal).

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka SSLC Science textbook Chapter 7.

resources.label: NCERT Science Textbook Class 10
resources.description: NCERT Class 10 Science Chapter 8 How do Organisms Reproduce?

resources.label: Reproduction — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on asexual and sexual reproduction.

pyqs.question: How is binary fission in Amoeba different from budding in Hydra?
pyqs.answer: Binary fission (Amoeba): mother cell divides equally into 2 daughter cells. Budding (Hydra): small bud grows by cell division from a specific part, separates from mother body and grows into new organism.

pyqs.question: List 3 methods of contraception, function of vas deferens and prostate gland, and explain menstrual cycle.
pyqs.answer: Contraception: (i) Mechanical (condom, copper-T) (ii) Oral pills (hormone imbalance) (iii) Surgical (blocking vas deferens/fallopian tube). Vas deferens: transports sperm from testes. Prostate gland: provides fluid medium and nutrients for sperm. Menstrual cycle: if egg not fertilised, uterus lining breaks down as blood+mucus every ~28 days.

pyqs.question: What is the role of placenta in development of embryo? Why are testes located outside abdomen?
pyqs.answer: Placenta: transports nutrients from mother to foetus, transports waste from foetus to mother. Testes outside: sperm production requires temperature lower than body temperature — scrotum maintains this lower temperature.

pyqs.question: Write two differences between self and cross pollination. What changes occur in flower after fertilisation?
pyqs.answer: Self: pollen to stigma of same flower, more similar offspring. Cross: pollen to stigma of different flower, more variation, media needed. After fertilisation: zygote divides → embryo; petals/sepals fall; egg → seed with thick layer; ovary → fruit.

mcqs.question: In Amoeba, reproduction occurs by:
mcqs.explanation: Amoeba divides into two equal daughter cells by binary fission. The nucleus divides first (karyokinesis) then cytoplasm (cytokinesis).

mcqs.question: Testes are located outside the abdominal cavity because:
options: B. Sperm production requires lower temperature than body
mcqs.explanation: Normal body temperature (37°C) is too high for sperm production. Scrotum maintains 2-3°C lower temperature needed for spermatogenesis.

mcqs.question: Placenta functions include:
options: B. Transports nutrients to foetus and removes waste
mcqs.explanation: Placenta is the interface between mother and foetus — transfers O₂, nutrients from mother to foetus; CO₂, urea from foetus to mother.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT asexual reproduction?
mcqs.explanation: Cross pollination is sexual reproduction involving male (pollen) and female (ovule) gametes. Others are asexual.

mcqs.question: Vas deferens transports:
mcqs.explanation: Vas deferens (sperm duct) carries sperm from epididymis (where sperm mature) to urethra for ejaculation.

mcqs.question: The menstrual cycle occurs when:
mcqs.explanation: If egg not fertilised, thick uterus lining breaks down and flows out as blood and mucus every ~28 days.

mcqs.question: Fragmentation as a mode of reproduction is seen in:
mcqs.explanation: Spirogyra (green algae) reproduces by fragmentation — it breaks into pieces, each growing into a new organism.

mcqs.question: The structure X in rhizopus (having thick wall) that grows into new organism is:
mcqs.explanation: Rhizopus reproduces by spore formation. Spores have thick walls (resistant), produced in sporangia, spread by wind, germinate into new Rhizopus.

mcqs.question: After fertilisation in a flower, the ovary develops into:
mcqs.explanation: After fertilisation: ovary → fruit, ovule → seed, zygote → embryo. Petals, sepals, stamens fall off.

mcqs.question: Advantages of vegetative propagation include:
options: B. Plants produce flowers and fruit faster
mcqs.explanation: Vegetatively propagated plants flower and fruit quickly (no seed germination wait), genetically identical to parent, useful for plants not producing seeds.

mcqs.question: Regeneration in Planaria means:
options: B. Each cut piece grows into complete organism
mcqs.explanation: Planaria has high regenerative capacity. Each fragment regenerates missing parts to form a complete worm.

mcqs.question: Sexual reproduction creates more variation because:
options: B. DNA from two parents combines
D. It is faster than asexual
mcqs.explanation: Sexual reproduction combines DNA from two parents through fertilisation, creating new gene combinations → genetic variation → better adaptability.

mcqs.question: How many chromosomes are in gametes if zygote has 24 pairs?
mcqs.explanation: Gametes are haploid (n). Zygote has 24 pairs = 48 chromosomes. Gametes have 48/2 = 24 chromosomes.

mcqs.question: Copper-T is a method of contraception that:
options: B. Prevents implantation of fertilised egg
mcqs.explanation: Copper-T (IUD) is placed in uterus. It prevents implantation of fertilised egg. It also affects sperm motility.

mcqs.question: Double fertilisation in plants refers to:
options: A. Two pollen grains fertilise one egg
B. One pollen tube with two male gametes — one fertilises egg, other fuses with polar nuclei
mcqs.explanation: In angiosperms: one male gamete + egg → zygote. Second male gamete + two polar nuclei → endosperm (3n). This is double fertilisation, unique to flowering plants.

exercises.question: Distinguish between asexual and sexual reproduction with examples.
exercises.answer: Asexual: one parent, no gametes, offspring identical to parent, no variation. Examples: binary fission (Amoeba), budding (Hydra), vegetative propagation (potato). Sexual: two parents, gametes fuse, offspring have variation. Examples: flowers, humans.

exercises.question: Explain the process of pollination and fertilisation in a flower.
exercises.answer: Pollination: transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. Self-pollination (same flower) or cross-pollination (different flower — by wind, water, insects, birds). Fertilisation: pollen lands on stigma → pollen tube grows down style → male gamete enters ovule → fuses with egg (fertilisation) → zygote → embryo.

exercises.question: Describe the menstrual cycle.
exercises.answer: Day 1-5: menstruation (uterus lining sheds). Day 6-13: follicle develops in ovary, uterus lining builds up. Day 14: ovulation (egg released). Day 15-28: if egg not fertilised, lining breaks down → menstruation starts again. If fertilised, implantation occurs → no menstruation (pregnancy).

exercises.question: What are the advantages of vegetative propagation?
exercises.answer: (i) Plants flower and fruit earlier (ii) Genetically identical (clones) — preserves good traits (iii) Useful for plants that do not produce seeds (banana) (iv) Large number of plants in less time (v) Disease-free plants can be maintained.

exercises.question: How does the number of chromosomes get re-established in sexual reproduction?
exercises.answer: In meiosis, sex cells (gametes) are formed with half (haploid, n) the normal number of chromosomes. When male gamete (n) + female gamete (n) fuse during fertilisation → zygote (2n) with original number restored. Example: Human gametes have 23 chromosomes; zygote has 46.

exercises.question: List four advantages of sexual reproduction over asexual.
exercises.answer: (i) Creates genetic variation → adaptability to changing environment (ii) Eliminates harmful mutations over generations (iii) Drives evolution (iv) Produces more diverse offspring better suited for survival.

exercises.question: Describe Hydra reproduction by budding.
exercises.answer: Hydra reproduces asexually by budding. A small bud appears on the body of parent Hydra due to repeated cell division. The bud grows, develops a mouth and tentacles. When fully mature, it detaches from the parent and becomes a new independent Hydra.

exercises.question: Explain the changes that occur in a flower after fertilisation.
exercises.answer: After double fertilisation: (i) Zygote divides to form embryo (ii) Ovule develops thick layer → becomes seed (iii) Ovary grows, matures → fruit (iv) Petals, sepals, stamens, stigma and style fall off (v) Endosperm (from secondary fertilisation) provides food for growing embryo.
